title: Heat Exchangers: LMTD and ε-NTU
summary: Learn to analyse heat exchangers from scratch: per-stream energy balance and capacity rates, parallel vs counter flow temperature profiles, the LMTD method with the correct $\Delta T$ pairing, building the overall coefficient $U$, and the effectiveness-NTU method ($\varepsilon$, $NTU$, $C_R$) for inlet-only problems. Includes fully worked tutorial problems and the rule for choosing LMTD vs $\varepsilon$-NTU.
objectives: Write the per-stream energy balance $q=\dot m\,c_p\,\Delta T$ and compute capacity rates $C=\dot m\,c_p$, identifying $C_{min}$ and $C_{max}$
Sketch parallel and counter flow temperature profiles and define $\Delta T_1,\Delta T_2$ correctly for each
Apply the LMTD method $q=U\,A\,\Delta T_{lm}$ to size area, length, or $U$
Build the overall heat transfer coefficient $U$ from series convection/conduction resistances
Use the $\varepsilon$-NTU method ($\varepsilon$, $NTU=UA/C_{min}$, $C_R=C_{min}/C_{max}$) for inlet-only problems
Apply the correct $\varepsilon$-NTU relations and special cases ($C_R=1$ counter flow, $C_R=0$ phase change)
Decide when to use LMTD versus $\varepsilon$-NTU

blocks.text: What a heat exchanger does

A **heat exchanger** is a device that transfers heat from a hot fluid to a cold fluid without mixing them. Think of a car radiator, a boiler, or the condenser of a power plant.

Our job is to connect five quantities:
- the two mass flow rates $\dot m_h$ and $\dot m_c$,
- the four inlet/outlet temperatures $T_{hi},T_{ho},T_{ci},T_{co}$,
- the overall heat transfer coefficient $U$,
- the heat transfer area $A$,
- the total heat rate $q$.

Two methods do this: the **LMTD method** (when you know the temperatures) and the **$\varepsilon$-NTU method** (when you only know inlet temperatures).

- **Parallel flow**: both fluids enter at the same end and flow the same direction. The cold outlet can never exceed the hot outlet.
- **Counter flow**: fluids enter at opposite ends and flow in opposite directions. Here the cold outlet **can** exceed the hot outlet ($T_{co}>T_{ho}$ is allowed).
- **Cross flow** and **shell-and-tube**: more complex; handled with a correction factor $F$.
Energy balance and capacity rates

Assume steady state, no shaft work, no heat loss to surroundings, negligible kinetic/potential energy, constant pressure, constant $c_p$. The first law for each stream gives the heat each fluid gives up or absorbs. The hot fluid **loses** heat (its $T$ drops); the cold fluid **gains** the same heat (its $T$ rises).

blocks.caption: Same $q$ for both streams (energy is conserved). Always use $c_p$, never $c_v$.

blocks.text:
The **capacity rate** is the heat needed to change a stream's temperature by 1 K per unit time:
$$C_h=\dot m_h c_{ph},\qquad C_c=\dot m_c c_{pc}\quad[\text{W/K}]$$
Then $q=C_h(T_{hi}-T_{ho})=C_c(T_{co}-T_{ci})$. The stream with the **smaller** $C$ undergoes the **larger** temperature change. We label the smaller one $C_{min}$ and the larger $C_{max}$.

blocks.title: Which fluid changes temperature more?
blocks.text: The stream with the small capacity rate swings more in temperature. In a **condenser** the hot fluid condenses at constant $T$, so $C_h\to\infty$ ($C_h\gg C_c$). In an **evaporator** the cold fluid boils at constant $T$, so $C_c\to\infty$ ($C_c\gg C_h$). In both, the phase-change side has $C_{max}$ and the other side is $C_{min}$.
Temperature profiles: parallel vs counter flow

The local temperature difference $\Delta T=T_h-T_c$ drives the heat transfer, but it changes along the exchanger, so we cannot just use one $\Delta T$.

- **Parallel flow**: $\Delta T$ is huge at the inlet and shrinks toward the outlet, approaching zero. The two outlet temperatures converge but never cross.
- **Counter flow**: $\Delta T$ is more uniform along the length. This is why counter flow is more effective for the same temperatures.

blocks.title: Parallel vs counter flow profiles
blocks.caption: Slide the inlet temperatures and capacity rates. Watch how $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ are defined at the two ends, and how counter flow keeps $\Delta T$ flatter and gives a larger $\Delta T_{lm}$.

blocks.text: The LMTD method

Integrating the differential energy balance along the exchanger shows the correct average $\Delta T$ to use in $q=UA\,\Delta T_m$ is the **log mean temperature difference** (LMTD), not the arithmetic mean.

blocks.tex: q=U\,A\,\Delta T_{lm},\qquad \Delta T_{lm}=\frac{\Delta T_1-\Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}
blocks.caption: $U$ = overall heat transfer coefficient $[\mathrm{W/m^2K}]$, $A$ = heat transfer area $[\mathrm{m^2}]$.

blocks.title: Define ΔT1 and ΔT2 correctly — the favourite mistake
blocks.text:
The end differences depend on the arrangement:
- **Parallel flow**: $\Delta T_1=T_{hi}-T_{ci}$ (inlet end), $\Delta T_2=T_{ho}-T_{co}$ (outlet end).
- **Counter flow**: $\Delta T_1=T_{hi}-T_{co}$, $\Delta T_2=T_{ho}-T_{ci}$.

For the same four temperatures, $\Delta T_{lm,CF}>\Delta T_{lm,PF}$, so counter flow needs **less area** for the same $q$. Mixing up these pairings is the single most common error.

Heat crosses convection (hot fluid $\to$ wall), conduction (through wall), convection (wall $\to$ cold fluid). These resistances add:
$$\frac{1}{UA}=R_{conv,h}+R_{cond}+R_{conv,c}=\frac{1}{h_iA_i}+\frac{\ln(r_e/r_i)}{2\pi Lk}+\frac{1}{h_eA_e}$$
Because inner and outer areas differ, $UA$ is the unambiguous quantity: $UA=U_iA_i=U_eA_e$. For a thin tube where $A_i\approx A_e$, $\;U=\big(1/h_i+1/h_e\big)^{-1}$.

steps.text: Energy balance: from the known stream, compute $q=\dot m\,c_p\,\Delta T$. Use it to find any missing outlet temperature on the other stream.
Identify the arrangement and write $\Delta T_1,\Delta T_2$ at the two ends with the correct pairing.
Compute $\Delta T_{lm}=(\Delta T_1-\Delta T_2)/\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)$.
Apply $q=UA\,\Delta T_{lm}$ to solve for the unknown ($A$, or $U$, or $L$ via $A=\pi D L$).
For cross/shell-and-tube, multiply by correction factor: $q=UAF\,\Delta T_{lm,CF}$, with $F=F(R,P)$ from charts.

blocks.title: Worked LMTD (Tutorial 8.1)
blocks.text:
**Given (parallel flow):** $\dot m_c=2\ \mathrm{kg/s}$, $c_{pc}=1500\ \mathrm{J/kgK}$, $T_{ci}=5$°C; $\dot m_h=0.3\ \mathrm{kg/s}$, $c_{ph}=4186\ \mathrm{J/kgK}$, $T_{hi}=80$°C, $T_{ho}=40$°C; $U=85\ \mathrm{W/m^2K}$. Find $A_{PF}$ and $A_{CF}$.

**Capacity rates:** $C_h=0.3\times4186=1255.8\ \mathrm{W/K}=C_{min}$; $C_c=2\times1500=3000\ \mathrm{W/K}=C_{max}$.

**Heat rate:** $q=C_h(T_{hi}-T_{ho})=1255.8\times(80-40)=50{,}232\ \mathrm{W}$.

**Cold outlet:** $T_{co}=T_{ci}+q/C_c=5+50232/3000=21.7$°C.

**Parallel:** $\Delta T_1=80-5=75$°C, $\Delta T_2=40-21.7=18.3$°C. $\Delta T_{lm,PF}=(75-18.3)/\ln(75/18.3)=40.2$°C. So $A_{PF}=q/(U\,\Delta T_{lm})=50232/(85\times40.2)=14.7\ \mathrm{m^2}$.

**Counter:** $q$ and $T_{co}$ are unchanged. $\Delta T_1=80-21.7=58.3$°C, $\Delta T_2=40-5=35$°C. $\Delta T_{lm,CF}=(58.3-35)/\ln(58.3/35)=45.7$°C. $A_{CF}=50232/(85\times45.7)=12.9\ \mathrm{m^2}$.

**Result:** $A_{CF}=12.9\ \mathrm{m^2}<A_{PF}=14.7\ \mathrm{m^2}$ — counter flow needs less area, as expected.
Inline check on ΔT pairing.

question.prompt: A **counter flow** exchanger has $T_{hi}=120$°C, $T_{ho}=60$°C, $T_{ci}=20$°C, $T_{co}=80$°C. What are $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$?
question.explanation: Counter flow pairs $\Delta T_1=T_{hi}-T_{co}=120-80=40$°C and $\Delta T_2=T_{ho}-T_{ci}=60-20=40$°C. Both ends equal means $C_h=C_c$ and a constant $\Delta T$ profile, so $\Delta T_{lm}=40$°C too. B uses the parallel-flow pairing $T_{hi}-T_{ci}$. C and D mix hot-with-hot or arbitrary differences and ignore the counter-flow end-matching rule.
question.theory: For counter flow, $\Delta T_1=T_{hi}-T_{co}$ and $\Delta T_2=T_{ho}-T_{ci}$; equal end differences indicate balanced capacity rates.

blocks.text: The effectiveness-NTU method
When you know only the **inlet** temperatures (not the outlets), LMTD would require iteration. The $\varepsilon$-NTU method avoids that. It uses three dimensionless groups.

- **Effectiveness** $\varepsilon=\dfrac{q}{q_{max}}$, with $q_{max}=C_{min}(T_{hi}-T_{ci})$ (the most heat an infinitely long counter flow exchanger could move). $0\le\varepsilon\le1$.
- **Number of Transfer Units** $NTU=\dfrac{UA}{C_{min}}$ — a dimensionless size of the exchanger.
- **Capacity ratio** $C_R=\dfrac{C_{min}}{C_{max}}$, with $0\le C_R\le1$.

blocks.caption: $q_{max}$ always uses $C_{min}$ and the full inlet temperature gap $T_{hi}-T_{ci}$.

blocks.title: qmax uses Cmin, not Cmax
blocks.text: The maximum heat is limited by the fluid that changes temperature the most — the $C_{min}$ fluid. If you plug $C_{max}$ into $q_{max}$ you overestimate it and get $\varepsilon$ too small. Also $T_{hi}-T_{ci}$ uses the two **inlets**, the largest possible difference in the device.

Effectiveness also follows directly from the temperature change of the $C_{min}$ fluid:
- If $C_{min}=C_h$: $\;\varepsilon=\dfrac{T_{hi}-T_{ho}}{T_{hi}-T_{ci}}$.
- If $C_{min}=C_c$: $\;\varepsilon=\dfrac{T_{co}-T_{ci}}{T_{hi}-T_{ci}}$.

The denominator is always $T_{hi}-T_{ci}$; the numerator is the temperature change of whichever fluid is $C_{min}$.

blocks.caption: $\varepsilon$-NTU relations for concentric pipes. Parallel flow (left), counter flow (right).

blocks.title: Special cases worth memorising
blocks.text:
- **Counter flow with $C_R=1$** (balanced, $C_h=C_c$): $\;\varepsilon_{CF}=\dfrac{NTU}{1+NTU}$.
- **$C_R=0$** (one fluid changes phase, $C_{max}\to\infty$): both arrangements give $\;\varepsilon=1-e^{-NTU}$.
- For the **same NTU and $C_R$**, counter flow always gives the highest $\varepsilon$ of the standard arrangements.

steps.text: Pick the right $\varepsilon$-NTU relation for the arrangement and evaluate $\varepsilon$.

blocks.title: Worked ε-NTU (Tutorial 8.2)
blocks.text:
**Given (counter flow):** $\dot m_h=1.5\ \mathrm{kg/s}$, $c_{ph}=1883\ \mathrm{J/kgK}$, $T_{hi}=115$°C; $\dot m_c=0.75\ \mathrm{kg/s}$, $c_{pc}=4184\ \mathrm{J/kgK}$, $T_{ci}=40$°C; $U=340\ \mathrm{W/m^2K}$, $A=13\ \mathrm{m^2}$. Find $q$, $T_{ho}$, $T_{co}$.

**Capacity rates:** $C_h=1.5\times1883=2824.5\ \mathrm{W/K}=C_{min}$; $C_c=0.75\times4184=3138\ \mathrm{W/K}=C_{max}$. So $C_R=2824.5/3138=0.90$.

**NTU:** $NTU=UA/C_{min}=340\times13/2824.5=1.56$.

**Effectiveness (counter):** $\varepsilon_{CF}=\dfrac{1-e^{-1.56(1-0.9)}}{1-0.9\,e^{-1.56(1-0.9)}}=\dfrac{1-e^{-0.156}}{1-0.9e^{-0.156}}=0.62$.

**Heat:** $q=\varepsilon C_{min}(T_{hi}-T_{ci})=0.62\times2824.5\times(115-40)=131{,}300\ \mathrm{W}\approx131.3\ \mathrm{kW}$.

**Outlets:** $T_{ho}=T_{hi}-q/C_h=115-131339/2824.5=68.5$°C; $T_{co}=T_{ci}+q/C_c=40+131339/3138=81.8$°C.

Note $T_{co}=81.8$°C $>T_{ho}=68.5$°C — only counter flow allows this crossover.
When to use which method

blocks.title: LMTD vs ε-NTU
blocks.text:
- Use **LMTD** when you know at least **three of the four** temperatures (typical design: find the area for required outlet temps). It is direct.
- Use **ε-NTU** when you know **only the two inlet** temperatures plus $UA$ (typical rating: find how the exchanger performs). LMTD here would need iteration.

Both describe the same physics and must give the same answer if you have the same data.
Inline check on method choice and qmax.

question.prompt: Air ($C=330\ \mathrm{W/K}$) enters at 70°C; water ($C=840\ \mathrm{W/K}$) enters at 20°C. You know $UA$ but neither outlet temperature. Which is true?
question.explanation: With only inlet temperatures known, LMTD needs iteration, so ε-NTU is the right tool — eliminating A and D. $q_{max}$ must use $C_{min}=330\ \mathrm{W/K}$ (the air) and the inlet gap $70-20=50\ \mathrm{K}$, giving 16.5 kW. C wrongly uses $C_{max}=840\ \mathrm{W/K}$, overstating the ceiling.
question.theory: Choose ε-NTU when only inlets are known; $q_{max}=C_{min}(T_{hi}-T_{ci})$ always uses the minimum capacity rate.

blocks.title: Units and sign housekeeping
blocks.text:
- $c_p$ in J/kgK gives $C$ in W/K and $q$ in W; if $c_p$ is in kJ/kgK keep it consistent.
- Temperature **differences** are identical in °C and K, so LMTD and capacity-rate equations can use °C directly. But any **absolute** temperature (rare here, e.g. radiation) needs K.
- $q$, $\varepsilon$, and $T_{co}$ do **not** depend on flow arrangement when the same four temperatures are fixed — only the required area does.

practice.prompt: A cross flow exchanger has $A=5\ \mathrm{m^2}$. Air ($\dot m=0.3\ \mathrm{kg/s}$, $c_p=1100\ \mathrm{J/kgK}$) enters at 70°C and is cooled by water ($\dot m=0.2\ \mathrm{kg/s}$, $c_p=4200\ \mathrm{J/kgK}$) entering at 20°C. The exchanged power is 12 kW. The effectiveness is:
practice.explanation: $C_{air}=0.3\times1100=330\ \mathrm{W/K}=C_{min}$; $C_{water}=0.2\times4200=840\ \mathrm{W/K}$. $q_{max}=C_{min}(T_{hi}-T_{ci})=330\times50=16500\ \mathrm{W}$. $\varepsilon=q/q_{max}=12000/16500=0.727$. B uses $C_{max}$ (840) in $q_{max}$ giving 0.286. A and C come from dividing by area or wrong gaps and have no physical basis.

practice.prompt: The number of transfer units NTU is defined as:
practice.explanation: By definition $NTU=UA/C_{min}$ — a dimensionless exchanger size. A uses $C_{max}$ (wrong reference capacity). B is the capacity ratio $C_R$. D is the effectiveness $\varepsilon$.

practice.prompt: For the same four inlet/outlet temperatures, how do the log mean temperature differences compare?
practice.explanation: Counter flow keeps $\Delta T$ flatter along the length, giving a larger log mean difference, so $\Delta T_{lm,CF}>\Delta T_{lm,PF}$. Hence counter flow needs less area for the same $q$. A reverses it. C is false in general (they only coincide in the degenerate $C_h=C_c$ counter case). D is irrelevant — $\Delta T_{lm}$ depends on temperatures, not $U$.
practice.theory: $\Delta T_{lm,CF}\ge\Delta T_{lm,PF}$ for identical end temperatures; counter flow is more area-efficient.

practice.prompt: In a condenser the hot fluid condenses at constant temperature. Which statement is correct?
practice.explanation: Phase change at constant $T$ means the condensing (hot) side has effectively infinite capacity rate, $C_h\gg C_c$, so $C_{min}=C_c$ and effectiveness uses the cold fluid's rise. B describes an evaporator (cold side condensing). C is the balanced $C_R=1$ case, opposite of $C_R=0$ here. D wrongly uses $C_{max}$ in $q_{max}$.
practice.theory: Condensation: $C_h\gg C_c$, $C_R\to0$, $\varepsilon=1-e^{-NTU}$; effectiveness uses the $C_{min}$ (non-phase-change) fluid.

practice.prompt: For a counter flow exchanger with balanced capacity rates ($C_h=C_c$), the effectiveness is:
practice.explanation: When $C_R=1$ the counter flow relation reduces to $\varepsilon_{CF}=NTU/(1+NTU)$ (equivalently $1/(1+1/NTU)$). A is the $C_R=0$ limit (phase change). C and D are not valid effectiveness expressions for this case.
practice.source: Lecture HT3 — CF effectiveness with Ch=Cc

practice.prompt: Counter flow: $\dot m_h c_{ph}=2824.5\ \mathrm{W/K}$, $\dot m_c c_{pc}=3138\ \mathrm{W/K}$, $T_{hi}=115$°C, $T_{ci}=40$°C, $UA=4420\ \mathrm{W/K}$. The heat rate is closest to:
practice.explanation: $C_{min}=2824.5$, $C_R=0.90$, $NTU=4420/2824.5=1.56$. $\varepsilon_{CF}=(1-e^{-0.156})/(1-0.9e^{-0.156})=0.62$. $q=0.62\times2824.5\times75=131\ \mathrm{kW}$. B uses $q_{max}=C_{min}\Delta T$ with $\varepsilon=1$. C uses parallel-flow ε (smaller). D mistakenly uses $C_{max}$ in $q$.
practice.theory: $q=\varepsilon C_{min}(T_{hi}-T_{ci})$ with the counter-flow ε relation.

practice.prompt: Parallel flow: $\Delta T_1=75$°C at the inlet and $\Delta T_2=18.3$°C at the outlet. The LMTD is closest to:
practice.explanation: $\Delta T_{lm}=(75-18.3)/\ln(75/18.3)=56.7/\ln(4.10)=56.7/1.41=40.2$°C. A is the arithmetic mean wrongly applied to another problem. C is a miscomputed log. D is the numerator $\Delta T_1-\Delta T_2$, forgetting to divide by the log.
practice.theory: $\Delta T_{lm}=(\Delta T_1-\Delta T_2)/\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)$; the LMTD lies below the arithmetic mean.

practice.prompt: The maximum possible heat rate in any heat exchanger equals:
practice.explanation: $q_{max}=C_{min}(T_{hi}-T_{ci})$: the smallest-capacity fluid undergoes the largest possible temperature change (the full inlet gap). A overestimates with $C_{max}$. B uses outlet temperatures, which are unknown and smaller. D is the actual $q$ via LMTD, not the ideal maximum.
practice.theory: $q_{max}=C_{min}(T_{hi}-T_{ci})$, achieved by an infinitely long counter flow exchanger.

practice.prompt: In a counter flow exchanger it is possible that:
practice.explanation: Counter flow keeps a favourable $\Delta T$ everywhere, so the cold outlet can exceed the hot outlet ($T_{co}>T_{ho}$) — impossible in parallel flow. B violates the second law: the cold fluid can never exceed the hot inlet. C is impossible since $\varepsilon=q/q_{max}\le1$. D cannot happen because $\Delta T_{lm}$ is built from positive end differences.

practice.prompt: Parallel flow, $C_{min}=C_c=4186\ \mathrm{W/K}$, $C_{max}=C_h=12500\ \mathrm{W/K}$, $UA=3000\ \mathrm{W/K}$, $T_{hi}=250$°C, $T_{ci}=15$°C. The effectiveness is closest to:
The units of the overall heat transfer coefficient $U$ are:
practice.explanation: From $q=UA\,\Delta T$, $U=q/(A\Delta T)$ has units $\mathrm{W/(m^2\cdot K)}$. B is a thermal resistance. C is a heat flux. D is the inverse, i.e. an area-specific resistance.

practice.prompt: Which equation correctly combines the resistances for the overall coefficient of a tube exchanger?
practice.explanation: Resistances in series add, and $1/(UA)$ is the total resistance: $1/UA=R_{conv,h}+R_{cond}+R_{conv,c}$. A sets $UA$ (a conductance) equal to a sum of resistances — dimensionally wrong. C and D add conductances directly, which is only valid for parallel paths, not this series chain.

practice.prompt: You must size an exchanger to bring a fluid to a specified outlet temperature; you know three of the four temperatures and $U$. The most direct method is:

options.content: Neither; only charts can solve it

practice.explanation: With three temperatures known, you get the fourth from the energy balance, compute $\Delta T_{lm}$, and solve $q=UA\,\Delta T_{lm}$ for $A$ in one shot — that is the LMTD design method. A and C are for the rating problem where only inlets are known. D is false; the algebra is closed-form.
practice.theory: LMTD is direct when $\ge3$ temperatures are known; ε-NTU is for inlet-only data.

practice.prompt: Counter flow exchanger with $C_R=1$ has $UA/C_{min}=1$. Its effectiveness is:
practice.explanation: For $C_R=1$, $\varepsilon_{CF}=NTU/(1+NTU)=1/(1+1)=0.50$. B is $1-e^{-1}=0.63$, the $C_R=0$ result (wrong here). C is $e^{-1}$. D would require an infinitely large exchanger.

practice.prompt: In the energy balance $q=\dot m\,c_p\,\Delta T$ for a heat exchanger stream, the correct specific heat is:

options.content: $c_p$, since the process is at constant pressure

practice.explanation: Heat exchanger flows are modelled as steady, constant-pressure open systems, so the enthalpy change uses $c_p$. A is the closed-rigid-volume choice, not applicable to a flow device. B and D have no thermodynamic basis here.
practice.theory: Open SSSF, constant pressure: $q=\dot m\,c_p\,\Delta T$ (enthalpy-based).

practice.prompt: A thin-walled concentric tube has $h_i=2250\ \mathrm{W/m^2K}$ (water) and $h_e=38.8\ \mathrm{W/m^2K}$ (oil), wall resistance negligible. The overall coefficient $U$ is closest to:
practice.explanation: For a thin wall, $U=(1/h_i+1/h_e)^{-1}=(1/2250+1/38.8)^{-1}=(0.000444+0.02577)^{-1}=38.1\ \mathrm{W/m^2K}$. The small coefficient (oil) dominates the total resistance. A is the arithmetic mean. C is the sum. D is twice the smaller coefficient — none respect series resistance addition.
practice.theory: Series convective resistances: $U=(1/h_i+1/h_e)^{-1}$; the smallest $h$ controls $U$.

exam.title: Parallel vs counter flow sizing
exam.statement: A concentric-pipe heat exchanger uses hot water to heat a process fluid. Hot: $\dot m_h=0.3\ \mathrm{kg/s}$, $c_{ph}=4186\ \mathrm{J/kgK}$, $T_{hi}=80$°C, $T_{ho}=40$°C. Cold: $\dot m_c=2\ \mathrm{kg/s}$, $c_{pc}=1500\ \mathrm{J/kgK}$, $T_{ci}=5$°C. Overall coefficient $U=85\ \mathrm{W/m^2K}$. Find the required area for (a) parallel flow and (b) counter flow, and comment.
exam.finalAnswer: $A_{PF}=14.7\ \mathrm{m^2}$, $A_{CF}=12.9\ \mathrm{m^2}$. Counter flow needs about 12% less area because $\Delta T_{lm,CF}>\Delta T_{lm,PF}$ for the same temperatures.
exam.tips: Compute $q$ and the missing outlet first — they do not depend on arrangement. Only the $\Delta T_1,\Delta T_2$ pairing changes between PF and CF.

exam.title: Rating a counter flow exchanger with ε-NTU
exam.statement: A counter flow exchanger has $UA$ fixed by its geometry. Hot fluid: $\dot m_h=1.5\ \mathrm{kg/s}$, $c_{ph}=1883\ \mathrm{J/kgK}$, $T_{hi}=115$°C. Cold fluid: $\dot m_c=0.75\ \mathrm{kg/s}$, $c_{pc}=4184\ \mathrm{J/kgK}$, $T_{ci}=40$°C. $U=340\ \mathrm{W/m^2K}$, $A=13\ \mathrm{m^2}$. Only the inlets are known. Find $q$, $T_{ho}$ and $T_{co}$.

steps.title: Why ε-NTU
steps.content: Only inlet temperatures are known, so LMTD would need iteration. Use ε-NTU.

exam.finalAnswer: $q\approx131.3\ \mathrm{kW}$, $T_{ho}=68.5$°C, $T_{co}=81.8$°C. Note $T_{co}>T_{ho}$, possible only in counter flow.
exam.tips: $q_{max}$ uses $C_{min}$ and the inlet gap $T_{hi}-T_{ci}$. Check that the energy balance closes: $C_h\Delta T_h=C_c\Delta T_c$.

exam.title: Finding length from U and LMTD
exam.statement: Water is heated from $T_{ci}=20$°C to $T_{co}=70$°C by a hot stream cooling from $T_{hi}=150$°C to $T_{ho}=80$°C in a counter flow concentric tube of inner diameter $D_i$. Cold side $\dot m_c c_{pc}$ gives $q=105\ \mathrm{kW}$; $U=350\ \mathrm{W/m^2K}$. (a) Find the required area. (b) If instead it were parallel flow with the same temperatures, would the area be larger or smaller? Justify.
exam.finalAnswer: $A_{CF}=4.3\ \mathrm{m^2}$; parallel flow would need $6.4\ \mathrm{m^2}$ (larger), because $\Delta T_{lm,PF}<\Delta T_{lm,CF}$.
exam.tips: Length follows from $A=\pi D_i L$ once $A$ is known. Smaller LMTD always means more area for the same $q$ and $U$.

exam.title: Conceptual: profiles, methods and special cases
exam.statement: (a) Sketch and describe the temperature profiles of parallel and counter flow exchangers, defining $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ for each. (b) State when to use the LMTD method and when to use ε-NTU. (c) Explain what happens to $C_R$ and to $\varepsilon$ in a condenser, and give the limiting effectiveness relation.

steps.title: Profiles
steps.content: Parallel: both fluids start far apart and converge; $\Delta T$ is largest at the inlet and shrinks. $\Delta T_1=T_{hi}-T_{ci}$, $\Delta T_2=T_{ho}-T_{co}$. Counter: fluids flow oppositely, $\Delta T$ stays flatter; $\Delta T_1=T_{hi}-T_{co}$, $\Delta T_2=T_{ho}-T_{ci}$. Counter allows $T_{co}>T_{ho}$.

steps.title: Method choice
steps.content: LMTD: use when at least three of four temperatures are known (design/sizing); direct via $q=UA\,\Delta T_{lm}$. ε-NTU: use when only the two inlet temperatures and $UA$ are known (rating); avoids iteration via $q=\varepsilon C_{min}(T_{hi}-T_{ci})$.

steps.title: Condenser limit
steps.content: Hot fluid condenses at constant $T$, so $C_h\to\infty$, giving $C_R=C_{min}/C_{max}\to0$. Both PF and CF relations reduce to $\varepsilon=1-e^{-NTU}$. Effectiveness uses the cold (finite-$C$) fluid: $\varepsilon=(T_{co}-T_{ci})/(T_{hi}-T_{ci})$.

exam.finalAnswer: Counter flow gives a flatter $\Delta T$ and larger $\Delta T_{lm}$; use LMTD with $\ge3$ temperatures, ε-NTU with inlets only; a condenser has $C_R\to0$ and $\varepsilon=1-e^{-NTU}$.
exam.tips: On open questions, always define every symbol and state assumptions (steady, constant $c_p$, no losses). A clear labelled sketch earns marks.
